claims.flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with Section 7.02(b)(iii).
claims.notes: Claim captures the two primary conditions in Section 7.01(d)(i)-(ii) but omits the provisos in clauses (x) and (y), which state additional mandatory preconditions (compliance with Section 5.04 and payment of the Company Termination Fee) that limit the Company's termination right.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 5.03 No Solicitation. <omitted> Section 5.04 Company Recommendation. <omitted> (b) Superior Proposal. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this Agreement, at any time prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, the Company Board (or any duly authorized committee thereof) may, in response to the receipt of a bona fide, written Competing Proposal received after the date hereof that did not result from a breach of ​Section 5.03(a) and is not withdrawn, make a Change of Company Recommendation (and, if so desired by the Company Board (or any duly authorized committee thereof) terminate this Agreement in accordance with Section 7.01(d) in order to cause the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Competing Proposal), only if: <omitted> Section 7.01 Termination. This Agreement may be terminated, in the case of clauses (a), ​(b), (e), or ​(f) below, at any time prior to the Effective Time, whether before or after the Company Shareholder Approval or, in the case of clauses (c) or ​(d) below, at any time prior to receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, as follows: <omitted> (d) by the Company, at any time prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, if (i) the Company has received a Superior Proposal and (ii) the Company Board (or a duly authorized committee thereof) has authorized the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement concurrently with such termination in order to accept such Superior Proposal; provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under ​Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with ​Section 7.02(b)(iii). (Page 19)

Question:
Limitations on FTR Exercise-Answer

Llama answer:
The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

Canonical claims:
- claim-dbb3bb141b72: The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 5.03 No Solicitation. <omitted> Section 5.04 Company Recommendation. <omitted> (b) Superior Proposal. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this Agreement, at any time prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, the Company Board (or any duly authorized committee thereof) may, in response to the receipt of a bona fide, written Competing Proposal received after the date hereof that did not result from a breach of ​Section 5.03(a) and is not withdrawn, make a Change of Company Recommendation (and, if so desired by the Company Board (or any duly authorized committee thereof) terminate this Agreement in accordance with Section 7.01(d) in order to cause the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Competing Proposal), only if: <omitted> Section 7.01 Termination. This Agreement may be terminated, in the case of clauses (a), ​(b), (e), or ​(f) below, at any time prior to the Effective Time, whether before or after the Company Shareholder Approval or, in the case of clauses (c) or ​(d) below, at any time prior to receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, as follows: <omitted> (d) by the Company, at any time prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, if (i) the Company has received a Superior Proposal and (ii) the Company Board (or a duly authorized committee thereof) has authorized the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement concurrently with such termination in order to accept such Superior Proposal; provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under ​Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with ​Section 7.02(b)(iii). (Page 19)

Question:
Limitations on FTR Exercise-Answer

Llama answer:
The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

Canonical claims:
- claim-dbb3bb141b72: The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the Llama answer against the canonical claim using only the provided contract excerpt, question, model answer (Llama answer), and fixed claim list.

Let me break down the task:
1. For each claim (there's only one here: claim-dbb3bb141b72), I need to return:
   - correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
   - load_bearing_label: yes or no
   - flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
   - notes: optional short note

The claim is: "The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement."

The Llama answer is exactly the same as the canonical claim.

Now I need to check the contract excerpt to verify if this is accurate.

From the contract excerpt, Section 7.01(d):
"by the Company, at any time prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval, if (i) the Company has received a Superior Proposal and (ii) the Company Board (or a duly authorized committee thereof) has authorized the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement concurrently with such termination in order to accept such Superior Proposal; provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with Section 7.02(b)(iii)."

The claim states: "The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement."

This is essentially true based on the text, though it omits the additional conditions (compliance with Section 5.04 and payment of termination fee). However, the claim captures the core conditions. The question is whether this is "true" or "partially_true" since it omits the provisos.

Wait, looking at the claim and the Llama answer, they are identical. The claim is a simplified version of the termination right. Does the omission of the provisos make it partially true?

In legal annotation tasks like this, usually if the claim states sufficient conditions but omits necessary conditions (like the provisos), it might be partially true. But let me think carefully.

The claim says: "The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement."

The actual text says the Company may terminate "if (i) ... and (ii) ...; provided, however, that (x) ... and (y) ..."

So the claim omits two provisos: (x) compliance with Section 5.04 and (y) payment of termination fee. These are necessary conditions ("provided, however, that").

However, looking at the claim, it doesn't say "if and only if" - it just says "if". In natural language, saying "if A and B" when the actual requirement is "if A and B and C and D" can be misleading because it suggests A and B are sufficient when they are not.

But in many legal QA datasets, if the answer captures the main substantive conditions but omits procedural/ancillary conditions like fee payment, it might still be considered true if the question is about the general limitations. But here the question is "Limitations on FTR Exercise-Answer" (FTR likely means Fiduciary Termination Right or similar).

Actually, looking at the exact wording: The Llama answer matches the canonical claim exactly. So if the canonical claim is the ground truth, then the Llama answer is correct. But I need to evaluate the claim against the contract, not just compare Llama answer to claim.

Wait, the instructions say: "For each claim return: ..." and I have a canonical claim list. I think I need to evaluate whether the claim is correct based on the contract, and whether the Llama answer supports it.

Actually, re-reading: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." and "For each claim return: ..."

So I need to evaluate the claim (canonical claim) against the contract text, and also consider the model answer? Or is the task to evaluate whether the model answer correctly supports the claim?

Typically in these tasks:
- correctness_label: whether the claim is true according to the contract
- load_bearing_label: whether the claim is "load bearing" - i.e., whether the model answer's correctness depends on this specific claim, or whether flipping the evidence would flip the answer.

Wait, "flip_evidence_text" suggests: if the load_bearing_label is yes, provide the text that would flip the claim (i.e., contradict it).

Let me reconsider the task. It seems like this is from a legal NLP evaluation framework where:
- There are claims (decompositions of the answer)
- We need to label if each claim is correct according to the source text
- We need to label if the claim is "load bearing" - meaning if this claim is central to the answer
- If load bearing, provide the evidence text that would flip it (i.e., show it's false)

Given the Llama answer is exactly the same as the canonical claim, and there's only one claim, this claim is definitely load bearing because it's the entire answer.

Now for correctness: Is the claim true?

The claim: "The Company may terminate this Agreement prior to the receipt of the Company Shareholder Approval if it receives a Superior Proposal and the Company Board authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement."

Contract: Section 7.01(d) says the Company may terminate prior to receipt of Company Shareholder Approval if (i) received Superior Proposal and (ii) Board authorizes entering into binding definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement concurrently with termination... provided that (x) complied with Section 5.04 and (y) paid termination fee.

The claim omits the "provided however" conditions. Is this false, partially true, or true?

If someone says "You may drive if you have a license" when the actual rule is "You may drive if you have a license and are over 16 and not intoxicated", the statement "You may drive if you have a license" is technically true (it's a necessary condition, but not sufficient). However, in common parlance, when listing conditions for a right, omitting additional necessary conditions is often considered incomplete or partially true.

But in legal contexts, precision matters. The claim presents two conditions as the requirements for termination, but there are actually four conditions. This could be considered partially true because it identifies necessary conditions but not all of them, potentially misleading the reader into thinking those are the only requirements.

However, looking at the wording "if it receives... and... authorizes...", this doesn't explicitly say "if and only if" or "provided that these are the only conditions". It just states that termination is permitted under these circumstances. And indeed, if those two conditions are met (plus the others), termination is permitted. But if those two are met and the others aren't, termination is NOT permitted.

Actually, the claim says "if it receives... and... authorizes..." which implies sufficiency. If I say "You may enter if you have a ticket", I imply that having a ticket is sufficient. If you also need an ID, then my statement is misleading.

Given this is a legal contract and the omitted provisions (compliance with Section 5.04 and payment of termination fee) are substantive preconditions, the claim is at best partially true. But I need to check if in the context of this specific research pilot, they consider such omissions as true or partially true.

Given the strict nature of legal contracts, omitting material conditions like payment of a termination fee and compliance with Section 5.04 covenants makes the claim incomplete. Therefore, "partially_true" seems most appropriate.

Wait, but the Llama answer is identical to the claim. And the question asks about "Limitations on FTR Exercise". The answer captures the main mechanism but misses the limitations (the provisos). Since the question is specifically about limitations, omitting the provisos is particularly problematic.

Actually, looking more carefully at the contract text, Section 7.01(d) is the termination provision. The claim accurately reflects clauses (i) and (ii) but omits the provisos (x) and (y).

I think the safest label is "partially_true" because while the claim correctly identifies two necessary conditions, it fails to mention other necessary conditions, making it an incomplete and potentially misleading statement of the termination right.

But wait - is there any chance this should be "true"? If the question is just asking for the general conditions and the provisos are considered ancillary? No, payment of a termination fee and compliance with Section 5.04 are clearly material limitations on the exercise of the termination right.

Let me reconsider "load_bearing_label". The Llama answer consists entirely of this claim. So if this claim were false, the answer would be false. Therefore, it is load bearing: yes.

Flip evidence text: This should be the text from the contract that contradicts or qualifies the claim. Since the claim omits the provisos, the flip evidence would be the proviso language that shows additional required conditions. Or if the claim were false, it would be text contradicting it. Since it's partially true, the flip evidence might be the text that provides the missing nuance.

Actually, "flip_evidence_text" usually means: what evidence would flip the label from true to false? Or what evidence shows that the claim is not fully true? In this case, the proviso text shows that the claim is incomplete.

The proviso text: "provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with Section 7.02(b)(iii)."

This text shows that merely receiving a Superior Proposal and Board authorization is not enough; the Company must also comply with Section 5.04 and pay the termination fee.

So:
- correctness_label: partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with Section 7.02(b)(iii).
- notes: Claim omits material preconditions in the proviso.

Wait, I should double check if the claim is actually considered true in this context. The claim says "if it receives... and... authorizes...". The contract says "if (i)... and (ii)... provided however that (x)... and (y)...". 

In logic, "if A then B" is different from "if A and C then B". Here the contract says "if (i) and (ii) and (x) and (y), then termination permitted". The claim says "if (i) and (ii), then termination permitted". This is logically false as a statement of the rule, because (i) and (ii) alone are not sufficient.

However, sometimes in reading comprehension, extracting the main conditions without every caveat is considered correct. But given the specific instruction to use the contract excerpt and the legal nature, I think partially_true is correct.

Actually, looking at the exact wording again: The claim could be interpreted as "The Company may terminate ... if [among other things] it receives..." But that's a stretch. Usually, when asked about limitations and the answer omits key limitations (like having to pay a fee), it's incomplete.

Let me check if there's any other nuance. The claim says "authorizes the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement." The contract says "has authorized the Company to enter into a binding and definitive written Alternative Acquisition Agreement concurrently with such termination". The claim omits "concurrently with such termination" and "in order to accept such Superior Proposal". But these are less material than the provisos.

Also, the claim says "receives a Superior Proposal" while the contract says "has received a Superior Proposal". That's fine.

I think I'll go with partially_true.

One more thought: The question is "Limitations on FTR Exercise-Answer". The Llama answer gives conditions under which the Company MAY terminate, but doesn't mention the limitations (provisos). So it's actually a poor answer to the specific question asked, because it states the trigger but not the limitations. However, the claim itself is a factual statement about when termination is possible. Since it's possible only when those conditions are met (plus others), the statement isn't false, just incomplete.

I'll stick with partially_true, load_bearing=yes, and provide the proviso text as flip evidence.

Wait, I need to make sure about the format. The user wants for each claim:
- correctness_label
- load_bearing_label
- flip_evidence_text
- notes

I should probably format this clearly, perhaps as JSON or a structured list. The user didn't specify exact output format but listed the fields. I'll provide a clear structured output.

Let me reconsider the "flip_evidence_text". If load_bearing=yes, I need to provide the text that would flip the claim. Since the claim is partially true (not fully false), what does "flip" mean? Usually, flip evidence is the text that contradicts the claim. If the claim were "The Company may terminate if X", and the contract says "The Company may NOT terminate if not Y", then Y is the flip evidence. Here, the proviso text shows that additional conditions are required, which "flips" the implication that X alone is sufficient.

Actually, in some frameworks, flip evidence is the specific sentence or clause that, if interpreted differently or if present/absent, would change the truth value of the claim. Here, the proviso is the text that makes the claim only partially true instead of true.

I'll provide the proviso text.

Final check:
- correctness_label: partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that (x) the Company has complied with its covenants under Section 5.04 with respect to such Superior Proposal and (y) the Company has paid or concurrently pays the Company Termination Fee to Parent or its designee in accordance with Section 7.02(b)(iii).
- notes: Claim correctly identifies two necessary conditions but omits material provisos requiring compliance with Section 5.04 and payment of the Company Termination Fee.

This looks good.

Actually, in some frameworks, flip evidence is the specific sentence or clause that, if interpreted differently or if present/absent, would change the truth value of the claim. Here, the proviso is the text that makes the claim only partially true instead of true.
